stem: Two nonnegative numbers have a sum of \( [[s]] \). What is the maximum possible value of their product?
solution: Let the numbers be \( x \) and \( [[s]]-x \); the product is \( P(x)=x([[s]]-x) \). Solving \( P'(x)=[[s]]-2x=0 \) gives \( x=[[s]]/2 \), and \( P([[s]]/2) = \dfrac{[[s]]^{2}}{4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide the squared sum by 4, using \( s^2/2 \) instead.
This reports the optimal \( x=s/2 \) itself, not the maximum product.
This uses the sum itself squared without normalizing by the critical point.
This divides by 8 instead of 4.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A ball's height in meters at time \( t \) seconds is \( h(t) = -[[a]]t^{2} + [[b]]t + [[c]] \). What is the maximum height the ball reaches?
solution: Solving \( h'(t)=-2\cdot[[a]]t+[[b]]=0 \) gives the critical time \( t=\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} \). Evaluating \( h \) at this time gives the maximum height \( \dfrac{[[b]]^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]}+[[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the initial height \( h(0) \), not the maximum height.
This reports the time at which the maximum occurs, not the height itself.
This is a common sign/factor slip that drops the initial height \( c \) inconsistently across similar problems.
This forgets to divide by 4, using \( b^2/(2a) \) instead of \( b^2/(4a) \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The daily cost (in dollars) of running a production line at rate \( x>0 \) is \( C(x) = [[a]]x + \dfrac{[[b]]}{x} \). What is the minimum possible cost?
solution: Solving \( C'(x) = [[a]] - \dfrac{[[b]]}{x^{2}} = 0 \) gives the critical point \( x=\sqrt{[[b]]/[[a]]} \). Since \( C \) is convex for \( x>0 \), this is a minimum, and \( C\big(\sqrt{[[b]]/[[a]]}\big) = 2\sqrt{[[a]]\cdot[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the critical point \( x=\sqrt{ab} \)-style expression without the factor of 2 from evaluating \( C \) there.
This reports the optimal rate \( x=\sqrt{b/a} \) itself, not the minimum cost.
This drops the square root on \( a \), applying it only to \( b \).
This averages \( a \) and \( b \) as a product over 2 instead of using the calculus-derived formula.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A company's profit (in thousands of dollars) from producing \( x>0 \) thousand units is \( P(x) = -x^{3} + [[a]]x^{2} \). What is the maximum profit (for \( x>0 \))?
solution: Solving \( P'(x) = -3x^{2}+2\cdot[[a]]x = x(2\cdot[[a]]-3x) = 0 \) gives \( x=0 \) or \( x=\dfrac{2\cdot[[a]]}{3} \); the nonzero critical point is a maximum for \( x>0 \). Evaluating \( P \) there gives \( P\big(\tfrac{2\cdot[[a]]}{3}\big) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the critical point \( x=2a/3 \) itself, not the maximum profit.
This mistakes the coefficient \( a^{2} \) itself for the maximum profit value.
This uses the WRONG critical point \( x=a/3 \) instead of \( x=2a/3 \).
This has the right form but the wrong constant, from mis-simplifying the cubic evaluation.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
